name: Tagiri-bime
shortDescription: Tagiri-bime is a Japanese goddess, one of the three Munakata goddesses born from Susanoo's sword and enshrined at Munakata Taisha.
description: Tagiri-bime, also known as Tagitsu-hime, is a prominent goddess in Japanese mythology, primarily known as one of the three Munakata goddesses. These goddesses are daughters of Amaterasu, the sun goddess, and Susanoo, the storm god. According to the Kojiki, after Susanoo's tumultuous behavior in Takamagahara (the heavenly realm), Amaterasu challenged him to a trial to prove his sincerity. They each broke the other's sword and created deities from them. Tagiri-bime was born from Amaterasu chewing on the Totsuka-no-Tsurugi sword of Susanoo. She is primarily enshrined at the Munakata Taisha Grand Shrine in Fukuoka Prefecture, along with her sisters, Ichikishima-hime and Tagori-hime. Her role is associated with safe passage and protection, particularly for seafarers, and she is revered for her divine power and grace.
subtitle: Guardian of the Sea, Daughter of Swords

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Tagiri-bime's origin is deeply rooted in the mythological narrative of the confrontation between Amaterasu and Susanoo. The Kojiki details how these deities were born through the act of breaking and chewing swords, symbolizing a reconciliation after conflict. Tagiri-bime, along with her sisters, emerged from this act, marking her as a significant figure in the divine lineage. The myth highlights her connection to both Amaterasu and Susanoo, embodying aspects of both celestial light and tempestuous power. Tagiri-bime is enshrined at Hetsu-miya, one of the three main shrines that comprise Munakata Taisha, emphasizing her central role in the Munakata goddesses' collective worship.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: As one of the Munakata goddesses, Tagiri-bime holds a crucial position in Japanese maritime culture and religious practices. Her primary role is that of a guardian deity, ensuring the safety of those who travel by sea. Historically, the Munakata region served as a vital point for maritime trade and cultural exchange between Japan and the Asian mainland. Consequently, Tagiri-bime's protection was highly valued by sailors, merchants, and diplomats. Her shrine at Munakata Taisha became a focal point for prayers and offerings, seeking her blessings for secure voyages and prosperous ventures. Her significance extends beyond mere protection, encompassing themes of purification and spiritual guidance, crucial for navigating both physical and metaphorical seas.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Tagiri-bime's symbolism is intricately linked to the sea, travel, and the balance between opposing forces. Her birth from the broken sword suggests a reconciliation of conflict, indicating themes of peace and harmony achieved through negotiation. The sea itself represents both opportunity and danger, mirroring the challenges faced by those who traverse it. Thus, Tagiri-bime embodies the hope for safe passage and successful navigation through life's uncertainties. Her role as a guardian deity also reflects the importance of respect for nature and the need for divine intervention to overcome its inherent risks. In a broader sense, Tagiri-bime symbolizes the interconnectedness of opposing forces and the potential for creation and protection to emerge from conflict.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tagiri-bime's legacy persists in modern Japanese culture through continued worship at Munakata Taisha and other affiliated shrines. The Munakata region remains a site of pilgrimage, attracting visitors who seek blessings for safe travel and prosperity. The goddess's image is frequently invoked in maritime safety campaigns and transportation-related advertisements, reinforcing her role as a protector of seafarers. Furthermore, her story continues to inspire artistic and literary works, perpetuating her mythological significance. The enduring reverence for Tagiri-bime underscores the enduring connection between Japanese society and the sea, as well as the enduring power of mythological figures to shape cultural values and beliefs.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The Kojiki is an early Japanese chronicle of myths, legends, and oral traditions, which provides the primary account of Tagiri-bime's birth and role.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: English translation of the Kojiki, providing insight into the original text and its cultural context.

associations.name: Sea
associations.significance: Tagiri-bime is a sea goddess, associated with maritime safety and navigation.

associations.name: Protection
associations.significance: She provides protection to seafarers and travelers.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Amphitrite
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Amphitrite is the Greek goddess of the sea and the wife of Poseidon, sharing a similar association with the ocean and maritime affairs.

companions.name: Ichikishima-hime
companions.description: One of the three Munakata goddesses, sharing a common origin and role.

companions.name: Tagori-hime
companions.description: Another of the three Munakata goddesses, also born from Susanoo's sword.

companions.name: Amaterasu
companions.description: Sun goddess and one of Tagiri-bime's parents, a major deity in Shinto.

companions.name: Susanoo
companions.description: Storm god and one of Tagiri-bime's parents, known for his turbulent nature.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings at Munakata Taisha
worshipPractices.description: Worshippers offer prayers and gifts at the shrine to seek Tagiri-bime's blessings for safe travel and prosperity.

worshipPractices.name: Purification rituals
worshipPractices.description: Rituals performed to cleanse and purify oneself before approaching the goddess, ensuring reverence and respect.

festivals.name: Munakata Taisha Grand Festival
festivals.description: Annual festival celebrating the Munakata goddesses, featuring traditional music, dance, and ceremonies.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tagiri-bime continues to be revered as a guardian of the sea, influencing maritime safety practices and cultural identity in the Munakata region and beyond. Her presence is felt in local folklore, art, and religious customs.
